gold_final_answer: Let r = max{p, q}. The number is s_n = ((n+1)(n+2))/2 for n < r, and s_n = (r(2n - r + 3))/2 for n ≥ r.
topics_flat: Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Sequences and Series > Telescoping series
prompt: Let $p$ and $q$ be two coprime positive integers, and let $n$ be a nonnegative integer. Determine the number of integers that can be written in the form $ip + jq$, where $i$ and $j$ are nonnegative integers with $i + j \le n$. (posed by Li Weigu)
response_text:
We count the distinct integers of the form
\[
ip+jq\qquad (i,j\ge 0,\ i+j\le n),
\]
where \(\gcd(p,q)=1\).

Let
\[
S_n=\{ip+jq:\ i,j\in \mathbb Z_{\ge0},\ i+j\le n\}.
\]
We want \(|S_n|\).

## 1. Reparametrize
Put \(k=i+j\). Then \(0\le k\le n\), and
\[
ip+jq=i p+(k-i)q=kq+i(p-q),
\]
with \(0\le i\le k\).

So for each fixed \(k\), we get the \(k+1\) numbers
\[
kq,\ kq+(p-q),\ kq+2(p-q),\dots,kq+k(p-q).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
S_n=\{\,kq+t(p-q): 0\le t\le k\le n\,\}.
\]

## 2. When can two representations coincide?
Suppose
\[
ip+jq=i'p+j'q
\]
with \(i+j\le n,\ i'+j'\le n\). Rearranging,
\[
(i-i')p=(j'-j)q.
\]
Since \(\gcd(p,q)=1\), we must have
\[
i-i'=tq,\qquad j'-j=tp
\]
for some integer \(t\). Hence
\[
(i'+j')-(i+j)=t(q-p).
\]
So collisions occur precisely by shifting
\[
(i,j)\mapsto (i-q,\ j+p)
\]
or the reverse, whenever coordinates stay nonnegative.

Thus the key parameter is \(p+q\): if \(i+j<n<p+q\), no such nontrivial shift can stay inside the triangle \(i+j\le n\).

Indeed, if \(ip+jq=i'p+j'q\) and \((i,j)\ne(i',j')\), then \(|t|\ge1\), so
\[
(i+j)+(i'+j')\ge p+q,
\]
and in particular one of the sums is at least \(p+q\).

Therefore:

- If \(n\le p+q-1\), all pairs \((i,j)\) with \(i+j\le n\) give distinct values.

The number of such pairs is
\[
1+2+\cdots+(n+1)=\frac{(n+1)(n+2)}2.
\]

## 3. For large \(n\): a canonical form
Using the move
\[
(i,j)\sim(i-q,\ j+p),
\]
every representable number has a unique representative with
\[
0\le i\le q-1,
\]
provided \(i+j\le n\).

For fixed \(i\in[0,q-1]\), \(j\) can range from \(0\) to \(n-i\), giving \(n-i+1\) values, as long as \(i\le n\).

Hence if \(n\ge q-1\), this gives
\[
|S_n|=\sum_{i=0}^{q-1}(n-i+1)=q(n+1)-\frac{q(q-1)}2,
\]
provided this canonical reduction is valid for every class. By symmetry we may also reduce to \(0\le j\le p-1\), giving
\[
|S_n|=p(n+1)-\frac{p(p-1)}2
\]
when \(n\ge p-1\).

These must agree only after all collisions are fully accounted for; the correct stable count is obtained by taking the smaller modulus direction, i.e. using \(m=\min(p,q)\).

Assume \(p\le q\). Then reducing \(j\) modulo \(p\) is the convenient choice: every value has a unique representation with
\[
0\le j\le p-1.
\]
For each \(j=0,1,\dots,p-1\), \(i\) may range from \(0\) to \(n-j\), giving \(n-j+1\) values (when \(j\le n\)). If \(n\ge p-1\), all \(j=0,\dots,p-1\) occur, so
\[
|S_n|=\sum_{j=0}^{p-1}(n-j+1)
= p(n+1)-\frac{p(p-1)}2.
\]
Since \(p=\min(p,q)\), this is
\[
|S_n|=m(n+1)-\frac{m(m-1)}2,\qquad m=\min(p,q),
\]
for \(n\ge m-1\).

Combining with the small-\(n\) case, the threshold is exactly \(n<m\): then no collisions occur, while for \(n\ge m-1\) the above formula holds. These two formulas agree at the boundary.

So in general,
\[
|S_n|
=\sum_{k=0}^{\min(n,m-1)}(n-k+1),
\qquad m=\min(p,q).
\]
Evaluating:

- If \(n\le m-1\),
\[
|S_n|=\frac{(n+1)(n+2)}2.
\]

- If \(n\ge m-1\),
\[
|S_n|=m(n+1)-\frac{m(m-1)}2.
\]

Equivalently,
\[
|S_n|=
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{(n+1)(n+2)}2, & n<\min(p,q),\\[6pt]
\min(p,q)(n+1)-\dfrac{\min(p,q)(\min(p,q)-1)}2, & n\ge \min(p,q).
\end{cases}
\]

Final answer: \[
\boxed{
\#\{ip+jq:\ i,j\ge0,\ i+j\le n\}=
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{(n+1)(n+2)}2, & n<\min(p,q),\\[6pt]
\min(p,q)(n+1)-\dfrac{\min(p,q)(\min(p,q)-1)}2, & n\ge \min(p,q).
\end{cases}}
\]
